_meta.authoringNotes: Citations cross-verified against ESMO 2020 Mesothelioma guidelines (Scherpereel PMID 31879258), CheckMate 743 trial (Baas Lancet 2021), Carbone CA Cancer J Clin 2019 (PMID 31283845), and 2024 NCCN Mesothelioma guidelines. Numbers checked against SEER 2024 release.

h1Title: Mesothelioma: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Mesothelioma is an aggressive cancer of the mesothelial cells that line the pleura, peritoneum, pericardium, or tunica vaginalis, with roughly 80% of cases linked directly to prior asbestos exposure and a typical latency of 30-40 years between exposure and diagnosis. Around 30,000 new cases occur globally each year and roughly 3,300 annually in the United States, with pleural mesothelioma accounting for 80-85% of presentations. Symptoms emerge insidiously — breathlessness from a pleural effusion, chest wall pain, or unexplained weight loss — and most patients are diagnosed at an advanced stage (60-70% stage III-IV). Historic 5-year survival was below 10%, but the CheckMate 743 trial of nivolumab plus ipilimumab raised median overall survival to 18.1 months in non-epithelioid disease. Treatment combines surgery, chemotherapy, and immunotherapy selected by histology and stage.

keyStats.prevalence: Roughly 3,300 new US cases per year (SEER 2024); 30,000 globally
keyStats.demographics: Men 4-5x more affected than women; median age at diagnosis 72 years
keyStats.avgAge: Most diagnoses occur age 65-79 after 30-40 year latency from asbestos exposure
keyStats.globalCases: Highest national incidence rates in the UK, Australia, Italy, and Netherlands

definition: Mesothelioma (ICD-10: C45) is a malignant neoplasm arising from the mesothelial cells that form serosal membranes — primarily the pleura around the lungs, the peritoneum around abdominal organs, the pericardium around the heart, and the tunica vaginalis around the testes. The dominant carcinogen is asbestos, and to a lesser extent erionite and therapeutic ionizing radiation. Inhaled or ingested mineral fibers translocate to mesothelial surfaces, where they generate chronic inflammation, reactive oxygen species, and somatic mutations in tumor-suppressor genes including BAP1, NF2, CDKN2A, and TP53. Loss of BAP1 is found in roughly 60% of pleural mesotheliomas and confers an inherited susceptibility syndrome in carriers of germline BAP1 mutations. Three histologic subtypes drive prognosis: epithelioid (60-70% of cases, best survival), sarcomatoid (10-20%, worst survival), and biphasic (10-15%, intermediate). Staging follows the AJCC 8th edition TNM system for pleural disease; peritoneal mesothelioma uses the PCI (Peritoneal Cancer Index) for surgical candidates. The disease is managed by thoracic oncologists, medical oncologists, surgical oncologists, and palliative-care teams in coordinated multidisciplinary clinics.

typesClassification.description: Arises from the lining of the lungs; accounts for 80-85% of all mesotheliomas. Presents with dyspnea, chest pain, and recurrent pleural effusion.

Arises from the abdominal lining; 10-20% of cases. Presents with ascites, abdominal pain, and weight loss. Cytoreductive surgery with hyperthermic intraperitoneal chemotherapy (CRS-HIPEC) offers the longest survival for selected patients.
Rare (<1%). Presents with pericardial effusion, dyspnea, and constrictive features. Median survival under 6 months due to late diagnosis.
Extremely rare (<1%). Presents as a unilateral hydrocele or paratesticular mass; diagnosed at orchiectomy.
60-70% of cases. Median overall survival 18-24 months with modern systemic therapy; the histology most likely to benefit from cytoreductive surgery.
Sarcomatoid 10-20%, biphasic 10-15%. Median survival 8-12 months historically; CheckMate 743 showed disproportionate survival gain with immunotherapy in non-epithelioid disease.

primarySymptoms: Progressive shortness of breath that develops over weeks to months, usually from a pleural effusion compressing the underlying lung — the single most common presenting symptom of pleural mesothelioma.
Dull, persistent chest wall pain on the affected side that may worsen with deep breathing, coughing, or lying down, reflecting tumor invasion of the parietal pleura or chest wall.
Unexplained weight loss of more than 5% of body weight over 6 months, often accompanied by fatigue and reduced appetite.
Persistent dry cough that does not respond to inhalers or antibiotics, sometimes accompanied by hoarseness if the recurrent laryngeal nerve is involved.
Abdominal distention, ascites, and a feeling of fullness in peritoneal mesothelioma, often misattributed to ovarian or gastrointestinal cancer at first presentation.
Night sweats and low-grade fevers that mimic infection or lymphoma, present in roughly 30% of patients at diagnosis.
Palpable chest wall mass at sites of previous biopsy or chest drainage — mesothelioma seeds along needle tracks in roughly 10% of untreated cases.
Pericardial effusion presenting as dyspnea on exertion, raised jugular venous pressure, or pulsus paradoxus in the rare pericardial form.
Painless scrotal swelling or unilateral hydrocele in tunica vaginalis mesothelioma, often diagnosed only at surgical exploration.
earlyWarningSigns: Recurrent unilateral pleural effusion in a patient with prior occupational asbestos exposure
Pleural plaques discovered incidentally on chest imaging in a former shipyard, construction, or insulation worker
Persistent unilateral chest discomfort that does not resolve over 4-6 weeks in an asbestos-exposed adult
Slowly enlarging unilateral hydrocele in a man over 60 with prior asbestos exposure
Unexplained ascites with elevated mesothelin levels in serum or ascitic fluid
emergencySigns: Acute breathlessness with low oxygen saturation — large pleural effusion or tension physiology may require emergency drainage
Superior vena cava obstruction (facial swelling, distended neck veins, headache) from mediastinal extension — needs urgent oncology and radiation oncology input
Cardiac tamponade physiology (hypotension, muffled heart sounds, raised JVP) in pericardial disease — requires emergency pericardiocentesis
New severe chest pain with hemoptysis — exclude pulmonary embolism, which is more common in mesothelioma due to hypercoagulability
Spinal cord compression from vertebral metastasis — back pain with leg weakness or new bladder dysfunction needs MRI within 24 hours

causes.description: The dominant cause, responsible for roughly 80% of cases. Inhaled asbestos fibers — especially amphibole varieties (crocidolite, amosite) — translocate to the pleura, where they generate chronic inflammation, reactive oxygen species, and DNA damage over 30-40 years before tumors emerge.

Household contact with asbestos workers (laundered work clothes), residence near asbestos mines or factories, and naturally occurring asbestos in soil all contribute. Domestic exposure explains a disproportionate share of female cases historically reported as 'idiopathic'.

A naturally occurring fibrous zeolite found in volcanic rock in Cappadocia (Turkey) and parts of North Dakota. Some villages there have mesothelioma rates exceeding 50% of deaths, providing the strongest single environmental signal in the literature.

Prior thoracic radiation for Hodgkin lymphoma, breast cancer, or testicular cancer raises mesothelioma risk roughly 8-10 fold, with latencies of 15-30 years. Radiation-induced cases are rising as cancer survivors age.

BAP1 tumor predisposition syndrome (BAP1-TPDS) confers a markedly elevated lifetime risk of mesothelioma, uveal melanoma, renal cell carcinoma, and cutaneous melanocytic tumors. Carriers develop mesothelioma at younger ages (median 55) and with longer survival than sporadic cases.

Some early studies detected SV40 DNA in mesothelioma tissue, proposing a cofactor role with asbestos. Larger replicating studies and the IARC have concluded the evidence is not strong enough to classify SV40 as a human carcinogen, and a causal role remains contested.

riskFactors.description: Shipbuilding, insulation, construction, demolition, plumbing, boilermaking, asbestos mining, and brake-lining work carry the highest risk. Lifetime risk among heavily exposed insulation workers reaches 10%.
Spouses and children of asbestos workers face elevated risk from fibers carried home on clothing. Documented case-control odds ratios range from 4 to 10 for cohabitants of exposed workers.

Men have 4-5x higher incidence, reflecting historical occupational exposure patterns; the male-to-female gap is narrowing as occupational exposures decline and household exposures dominate among women.
Median age at diagnosis is 72 years. The long latency between exposure and tumor emergence concentrates cases in older adults; pediatric mesothelioma is exceptionally rare.

Heritable BAP1 loss confers a markedly elevated lifetime risk of mesothelioma (estimated 6-25% lifetime risk) plus uveal melanoma and renal cell carcinoma. Genetic testing is offered to early-onset cases or those with family clusters.
Therapeutic radiation for Hodgkin lymphoma, breast cancer, testicular cancer, or Wilms tumor raises mesothelioma risk roughly 8-10 fold, with latencies of 15-30 years.

Naturally occurring fibrous minerals in Cappadocia (Turkey), parts of North Dakota, and Biancavilla (Sicily) produce extraordinary local incidence rates, demonstrating that non-asbestos mineral fibers also cause mesothelioma.

Smoking alone does not cause mesothelioma but multiplies lung cancer risk in asbestos-exposed individuals roughly 50-fold versus non-smoker non-exposed controls. Smoking cessation does not eliminate established asbestos risk.

affectedDemographics: US incidence 1.0 per 100,000 in men, 0.2 per 100,000 in women (SEER 2024)
Global incidence highest in the UK (3.0 per 100,000), Australia, Netherlands, and Italy — countries with peak historic asbestos consumption
Median age at diagnosis 72; under 1% of cases occur before age 45 outside germline BAP1 carriers
Indian and other emerging-market incidence is underestimated due to underdiagnosis; asbestos remains in commercial use in many low- and middle-income countries
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis follows a stepwise pathway anchored in imaging-guided tissue sampling, because cytology alone is rarely sufficient and small biopsies are prone to misclassification. The typical patient presents with unilateral pleural effusion or chest pain; chest X-ray followed by contrast-enhanced CT chest is the first imaging step. CT findings suggestive of mesothelioma include nodular or circumferential pleural thickening (>1 cm), mediastinal pleural involvement, infiltration into fissures, and chest wall extension. Pleural effusion is drained therapeutically and cytology is sent, though cytologic diagnosis alone is positive in only 30-50% of cases and considered inadequate by 2020 ESMO and 2024 NCCN guidelines for treatment planning. The definitive diagnostic step is image-guided or thoracoscopic pleural biopsy — thoracoscopic (medical or surgical) sampling yields a diagnosis in over 90% of cases. Immunohistochemistry is essential: positive staining for calretinin, WT1, CK5/6, and D2-40 (podoplanin), with negative carcinoma markers (CEA, BerEP4, TTF-1, claudin-4), supports mesothelial origin. BAP1 loss and CDKN2A homozygous deletion (by FISH) help distinguish malignant mesothelioma from reactive mesothelial proliferation. Once diagnosed, staging includes contrast CT chest/abdomen/pelvis, PET-CT (for nodal and extrathoracic disease), and brain MRI when symptomatic. Pulmonary function tests and cardiac assessment guide surgical eligibility. For peritoneal disease, diagnostic laparoscopy with biopsy is preferred, and the Peritoneal Cancer Index (PCI) is calculated to plan cytoreductive surgery candidacy.

diagnosticTests.test: Contrast-enhanced CT chest, abdomen, and pelvis
diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line imaging to characterize pleural thickening, identify effusion, assess mediastinal and chest wall involvement, and detect extrathoracic spread.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard CT scan lasting 5-10 minutes with intravenous iodinated contrast. Reports are usually available within 24-48 hours. Mild flushing or metallic taste during contrast injection is common and brief.

diagnosticTests.test: Pleural fluid cytology and biomarkers (mesothelin, fibulin-3)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Initial sampling of pleural effusion; cytology alone diagnoses 30-50% of cases. Serum and pleural mesothelin levels support but do not replace histology.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Thoracentesis under ultrasound guidance withdraws 50-100 mL or more of pleural fluid through a thin needle after local anesthetic. The procedure takes 15-20 minutes; soreness for 24 hours is typical.

diagnosticTests.test: Thoracoscopic pleural biopsy (medical thoracoscopy or VATS)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold-standard tissue sampling; yields diagnosis in over 90% of cases and provides enough tissue for immunohistochemistry and molecular testing.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A day-case or short-admission procedure under sedation or general anesthesia. A small incision allows a camera and instruments into the pleural space; multiple biopsies are taken and a chest drain is left in place for 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects metabolically active disease beyond the primary site, evaluates mediastinal nodes, and identifies distant metastases. Used for staging and assessing treatment response.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A short fasting interval followed by an intravenous injection of FDG tracer. Imaging starts 60-90 minutes later and lasts about 30 minutes. No discomfort beyond the venous cannula.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Standard for suspected peritoneal mesothelioma. Allows direct visualization of peritoneal surfaces, multiple biopsies, and calculation of the Peritoneal Cancer Index for surgical planning.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A general anesthetic short-stay procedure with two or three small abdominal incisions. Most patients go home within 24 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Immunohistochemistry and molecular testing (BAP1, CDKN2A FISH, MTAP)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Distinguishes mesothelioma from adenocarcinoma metastases and from reactive mesothelial hyperplasia. BAP1 loss and CDKN2A homozygous deletion are specific for malignancy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed by the pathologist on biopsy material. Adds 5-10 working days to the report. No additional procedure required for the patient.

diagnosticTests.test: Pulmonary function tests and cardiac assessment
diagnosticTests.purpose: Determines surgical candidacy for extended pleurectomy/decortication or extrapleural pneumonectomy. Predicts post-operative function and cardiopulmonary reserve.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Spirometry, lung volumes, diffusion capacity, and exercise testing performed over 30-60 minutes in a respiratory laboratory. Often paired with echocardiography.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of mesothelioma is multimodal and stratified by histology, stage, performance status, and surgical candidacy. Frontline systemic therapy underwent a major shift after the CheckMate 743 trial (Baas Lancet 2021) showed nivolumab plus ipilimumab (1 mg/kg + 3 mg/kg every 3 weeks, then nivolumab maintenance) improved median overall survival to 18.1 months versus 14.1 months with platinum-pemetrexed chemotherapy, with the greatest benefit in non-epithelioid disease (sarcomatoid and biphasic). The 2020 ESMO guideline and 2024 NCCN guideline now list nivolumab plus ipilimumab as the preferred first-line systemic regimen for unresectable pleural mesothelioma. Platinum-doublet chemotherapy with cisplatin (75 mg/m2) plus pemetrexed (500 mg/m2) every 3 weeks for 4-6 cycles remains an alternative or is added for epithelioid disease where response rates are higher. Surgery is reserved for selected stage I-IIIA epithelioid disease in fit patients at high-volume centers; extended pleurectomy/decortication has largely replaced extrapleural pneumonectomy because the MARS trial showed no survival benefit and high morbidity from the more radical operation. Adjuvant hemithoracic intensity-modulated radiation therapy may be added after pleurectomy. For peritoneal mesothelioma, cytoreductive surgery with hyperthermic intraperitoneal chemotherapy (CRS-HIPEC) achieves median survival of 53 months in carefully selected patients, far exceeding systemic chemotherapy alone. Second-line therapy after platinum-doublet includes single-agent immunotherapy, vinorelbine, gemcitabine, or clinical trial enrollment. Palliative care is integrated from diagnosis, with talc pleurodesis or indwelling pleural catheters for recurrent effusion and opioid-based analgesia for chest wall pain. Multidisciplinary review at a high-volume mesothelioma center is associated with longer survival.

medicalTreatments.name: Nivolumab plus ipilimumab (240 mg every 2 weeks + 1 mg/kg every 6 weeks, or per CheckMate 743 schedule)
medicalTreatments.description: Dual checkpoint blockade approved as first-line therapy for unresectable pleural mesothelioma based on the CheckMate 743 trial. PD-1 plus CTLA-4 inhibition restores anti-tumor T-cell activity. Treatment is continued for up to 2 years or until progression or unacceptable toxicity.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Median overall survival 18.1 months versus 14.1 months with chemotherapy; particularly striking benefit in non-epithelioid disease (median OS 18.1 vs 8.8 months).

medicalTreatments.name: Cisplatin (75 mg/m2) plus pemetrexed (500 mg/m2) every 3 weeks for 4-6 cycles
medicalTreatments.description: Historical standard of care since the Vogelzang 2003 trial. Folinic acid and B12 supplementation are required to mitigate pemetrexed toxicity. Carboplatin substitutes cisplatin in patients with renal impairment or poor performance status.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Median overall survival 12.1 months versus 9.3 months with cisplatin alone (Vogelzang 2003); response rate 41% in epithelioid disease.

medicalTreatments.name: Bevacizumab added to platinum-pemetrexed (15 mg/kg every 3 weeks then maintenance)
medicalTreatments.description: VEGF inhibitor added in the MAPS trial. Used in patients without contraindications to bevacizumab (hypertension, bleeding, recent surgery). Maintenance bevacizumab continues until progression.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Median overall survival 18.8 months versus 16.1 months with chemotherapy alone (MAPS trial, Zalcman Lancet 2016).

medicalTreatments.name: Single-agent nivolumab (240 mg every 2 weeks) — second-line
medicalTreatments.description: Used after progression on platinum-pemetrexed in patients ineligible for combination immunotherapy. CONFIRM and MERIT trials supported activity in relapsed disease.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Objective response rate 11% in relapsed disease; median overall survival 9.2 months versus 6.6 months with placebo (CONFIRM trial).

medicalTreatments.name: Vinorelbine (25-30 mg/m2 weekly) — second-line
medicalTreatments.description: Single-agent vinca alkaloid used as a tolerable second-line option for patients unfit for further platinum or immunotherapy. The VIM trial confirmed activity in pretreated mesothelioma.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Response rate roughly 15%; median overall survival 9.3 months in selected second-line patients.

medicalTreatments.name: Hyperthermic intraperitoneal chemotherapy (HIPEC) with mitomycin or cisplatin
medicalTreatments.description: Delivered intra-operatively at completion of cytoreductive surgery for peritoneal mesothelioma. Heated chemotherapy circulates through the peritoneum at 41-43°C for 60-90 minutes to treat microscopic residual disease.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Median overall survival 53 months in selected peritoneal mesothelioma patients with complete cytoreduction; 5-year survival 47% (Yan JCO 2009).

surgicalOptions.name: Pleurectomy and decortication (P/D) or extended pleurectomy/decortication (EPD)
surgicalOptions.description: Lung-sparing operation that strips the parietal and visceral pleura along with all gross tumor while preserving the underlying lung. Performed at high-volume centers for selected stage I-IIIA epithelioid disease.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Median overall survival 18-25 months after EPD in selected patients; 30-day mortality 4-7%.

surgicalOptions.name: Extrapleural pneumonectomy (EPP)
surgicalOptions.description: Radical resection of pleura, lung, ipsilateral diaphragm, and pericardium. Use has declined sharply after the MARS trial showed no survival advantage over P/D and high morbidity. Reserved for highly selected younger patients in expert centers.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 30-day mortality 4-15% historically; not associated with improved overall survival compared with P/D in randomized data.

surgicalOptions.name: Cytoreductive surgery with HIPEC (CRS-HIPEC)
surgicalOptions.description: Standard of care for peritoneal mesothelioma when complete macroscopic resection is feasible. Cytoreduction is followed by heated intraperitoneal chemotherapy. Performed at specialist peritoneal-malignancy centers.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Median overall survival 53 months and 5-year survival 47% in completely cytoreduced patients (Yan JCO 2009).

surgicalOptions.name: Talc pleurodesis or indwelling pleural catheter (palliative)
surgicalOptions.description: Manages recurrent pleural effusion in patients not undergoing definitive resection. Talc pleurodesis fuses the pleural surfaces; indwelling catheters allow ambulatory drainage at home.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Talc pleurodesis succeeds in 70-90% of patients; indwelling catheters relieve symptoms in over 90%.

alternativeTreatments.name: Multidisciplinary palliative care from diagnosis
alternativeTreatments.description: Early integrated palliative care improves quality of life and survival in advanced thoracic malignancies (Temel NEJM 2010 evidence extrapolated). Mesothelioma symptom burden is high and benefits from specialist input.

whySeeSpecialist: Mesothelioma requires multidisciplinary care at a high-volume center because diagnosis is histologically nuanced, staging guides surgical eligibility narrowly, and contemporary immunotherapy and surgical-HIPEC regimens have significant learning-curve effects. Specialists coordinate thoracic surgery, medical oncology, radiation oncology, pathology, palliative care, and clinical trial access — survival is measurably longer at specialist centers.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a thoracic medical oncologist or surgical oncologist with mesothelioma-specific volume (at least 15-20 cases annually), institutional access to CheckMate 743-style immunotherapy regimens, on-site pleural service with thoracoscopy, and active enrollment in mesothelioma clinical trials. For peritoneal disease, seek a peritoneal-surface-malignancy center with established CRS-HIPEC programs.
preventionStrategies: Eliminate occupational asbestos exposure — primary prevention has reduced incidence in countries with full asbestos bans by roughly 50% over 30 years
Use certified abatement contractors for asbestos removal in older buildings; never disturb friable asbestos without proper PPE and containment
Wash work clothes separately and shower before leaving the worksite if asbestos exposure is possible — household contact spreads risk to family members
Screen germline BAP1 mutation carriers with annual whole-body imaging starting in early adulthood per surveillance recommendations
Advocate for national asbestos bans in jurisdictions where commercial use continues — over 60 countries still permit chrysotile asbestos in construction
lifestyleModifications: Stop smoking — does not reverse asbestos risk but eliminates the multiplicative lung cancer risk and improves chemotherapy tolerance
Maintain physical activity within tolerance — preserved performance status is a major prognostic factor and influences treatment eligibility
Address nutritional decline early; involve dietetics if weight loss exceeds 5% as cachexia worsens chemotherapy and immunotherapy outcomes
Engage early specialist palliative care — randomized data show improved symptom control and quality of life
Track symptoms with a daily diary to identify pleural effusion recurrence and pain progression promptly

recommended: High-protein meals (1.2-1.5 g/kg body weight per day) to offset cachexia during treatment
Small frequent meals with calorie-dense additions (olive oil, nut butters) when appetite is poor
Adequate fluid intake — 2 liters daily unless restricted — to support kidney function during cisplatin or pemetrexed
Dietitian-supervised oral nutritional supplements if intake is inadequate
Fresh fruit and vegetables for micronutrient support during prolonged systemic therapy
Folate-rich foods to complement pemetrexed B12 and folinic acid supplementation
avoid: Highly restrictive 'cancer diets' without dietitian oversight — calorie deficits worsen outcomes
Unpasteurized dairy and undercooked food during neutropenia-inducing chemotherapy
Grapefruit and grapefruit juice when on certain CYP3A4-metabolized targeted agents
Alcohol exceeding 1 unit daily during active chemotherapy — interferes with metabolism and worsens nausea
Smoking — undermines all systemic therapy and surgical recovery

exerciseGuidelines: Maintain regular low-to-moderate intensity activity within breathlessness tolerance. Walking 20-30 minutes most days, supervised pulmonary rehabilitation before surgery, and graded resistance training during treatment all preserve performance status and reduce treatment-related fatigue.
dailyManagement: Take prescribed antiemetics, supportive medications, and pemetrexed-related folate and B12 exactly as instructed
Monitor for new breathlessness, increasing chest pain, or rapid weight loss and report promptly between scheduled visits
Use ambulatory pleural drainage devices as taught if an indwelling catheter is in place
Plan rest periods around treatment days — fatigue often peaks 5-7 days after cisplatin or 2-3 weeks after immunotherapy
Engage with mesothelioma patient organizations early for emotional and practical support, including legal and compensation guidance where applicable
prognosis: Prognosis remains guarded but has improved meaningfully over the past decade. Historical 5-year survival for pleural mesothelioma was below 10% across all stages. With contemporary immunotherapy, median overall survival is 18.1 months in unresectable pleural disease (CheckMate 743), reaching 24-30 months in selected stage I-II epithelioid patients who complete trimodality therapy. Peritoneal mesothelioma fares better when amenable to cytoreductive surgery with HIPEC, with median survival of 53 months and 5-year survival of 47% in completely cytoreduced patients. Adverse prognostic factors include sarcomatoid histology (median survival 8-12 months), advanced stage, poor performance status, low albumin, high platelet count, and absence of surgical candidacy. Favorable factors include epithelioid histology, complete cytoreduction, BAP1-mutant tumors (paradoxically associated with longer survival), and access to immunotherapy and clinical trials. The variation across specialist centers is substantial — high-volume centers report up to 30% longer median survival, reinforcing the importance of multidisciplinary care.
recoveryTimeline: Systemic therapy is delivered over 4-6 months for first-line chemotherapy and up to 2 years for first-line immunotherapy. Pleurectomy/decortication requires 6-8 weeks for return to baseline activity, with full pulmonary recovery over 3-6 months. Peritoneal CRS-HIPEC involves 7-10 days of inpatient recovery and 4-6 weeks of restricted activity. Surveillance imaging (CT chest with or without PET) is performed every 3 months for the first 2 years, then every 6 months.
complications: Recurrent pleural effusion causing breathlessness — manageable with talc pleurodesis or indwelling pleural catheters
Chest wall invasion with severe neuropathic pain requiring opioid and adjuvant analgesia
Pericardial effusion or constriction in extensive pleural or pericardial disease — pericardiocentesis or pericardial window may be needed
Venous thromboembolism — mesothelioma confers a hypercoagulable state, with PE rates exceeding 10% over the disease course
Immune-related adverse events on checkpoint inhibitors — colitis, pneumonitis, hepatitis, endocrinopathy in roughly 30-40% on combination immunotherapy
Cisplatin-related nephrotoxicity, ototoxicity, and peripheral neuropathy with cumulative exposure

supportResources.name: Mesothelioma Applied Research Foundation
supportResources.description: US patient organization with helplines, treatment center locator, and clinical trial navigation services.

supportResources.name: Mesothelioma UK
supportResources.description: UK charity providing clinical nurse specialists, patient support, and information on legal compensation routes.

supportResources.name: Asbestos Diseases Foundation of Australia
supportResources.description: Patient advocacy and information service for asbestos-related disease in Australia.

confusedWithConditions.name: Lung Cancer (Non-Small Cell)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Lung cancer arises from bronchial or alveolar epithelium and typically presents with a discrete pulmonary nodule or mass on CT, with positive TTF-1 and napsin A immunohistochemistry. Mesothelioma arises from the pleural lining itself with circumferential pleural thickening, calretinin-positive and TTF-1-negative staining. Both can occur in asbestos-exposed individuals.

confusedWithConditions.name: Pneumonia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Pneumonia presents acutely with fever, productive cough, and consolidation on imaging that responds to antibiotics. Mesothelioma develops over months with insidious dyspnea, persistent unilateral pleural effusion that does not resolve with antibiotics, and characteristic nodular pleural thickening on CT.

confusedWithConditions.name: Tuberculosis (Pleural)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Tuberculous pleurisy can mimic mesothelioma with unilateral effusion and pleural thickening, especially in endemic regions. TB pleural fluid is lymphocyte-predominant with elevated adenosine deaminase (ADA > 40 U/L) and acid-fast bacilli or positive PCR. Mesothelioma fluid contains malignant mesothelial cells and is diagnosed by pleural biopsy.

confusedWithConditions.name: Asbestosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Asbestosis is interstitial fibrosis caused by asbestos with bibasilar reticular changes and restrictive lung function; it shares the exposure history but is not malignant. Pleural plaques (a marker of asbestos exposure) often accompany asbestosis and can be misread as mesothelioma — plaques are calcified, smooth, and bilateral, whereas mesothelioma is nodular and unilateral.

relatedConditions.name: Lung Cancer
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares asbestos exposure as a major risk factor; smoking multiplies lung cancer risk in asbestos-exposed individuals but does not cause mesothelioma.

relatedConditions.name: Asbestosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Marker condition of heavy asbestos exposure; carriers have elevated baseline mesothelioma risk and warrant surveillance.

relatedConditions.name: Pneumonia
relatedConditions.relevance: Common differential for new respiratory symptoms and effusion; mesothelioma is often misdiagnosed as pneumonia initially.

relatedConditions.name: Tuberculosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Important differential in endemic regions because TB pleurisy mimics malignant mesothelioma radiologically.

relatedConditions.name: Pleural Effusion
relatedConditions.relevance: Most common initial presentation of pleural mesothelioma; recurrent unilateral effusion warrants pleural biopsy.

faqs.question: What is mesothelioma?
faqs.answer: Mesothelioma is a cancer of the thin tissue lining the lungs (pleura), abdomen (peritoneum), heart (pericardium), or testes (tunica vaginalis). About 80% of cases are linked to asbestos exposure, typically 30-40 years before diagnosis.

faqs.question: Is mesothelioma always caused by asbestos?
faqs.answer: Roughly 80% of mesotheliomas are linked to prior asbestos exposure. Other established causes include erionite (a mineral fiber found in Cappadocia and parts of North Dakota), prior therapeutic radiation, and germline BAP1 mutation. A minority of cases occur without identifiable exposure.

faqs.question: How long after asbestos exposure does mesothelioma appear?
faqs.answer: Mesothelioma has one of the longest latencies of any cancer. The median interval between first asbestos exposure and diagnosis is 30-40 years, ranging from 15 to 60+ years. This long delay explains why incidence continues to rise in many countries decades after asbestos bans.

faqs.question: What are the first symptoms of mesothelioma?
faqs.answer: Pleural mesothelioma usually begins with progressive shortness of breath from a pleural effusion, dull chest wall pain, and unexplained weight loss. Peritoneal mesothelioma causes abdominal distention, ascites, and fullness.

faqs.question: How is mesothelioma diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis requires a pleural or peritoneal biopsy, usually obtained by thoracoscopy or laparoscopy. CT imaging shows characteristic nodular pleural thickening. Cytology of pleural fluid alone is insufficient.

faqs.question: What is the survival rate for mesothelioma?
faqs.answer: Historical 5-year survival was below 10%. Modern first-line nivolumab plus ipilimumab achieves median overall survival of 18.1 months. Peritoneal mesothelioma treated with cytoreductive surgery and HIPEC reaches median survival of 53 months and 5-year survival of 47% in selected patients.

faqs.question: What is the difference between mesothelioma and lung cancer?
faqs.answer: Mesothelioma arises from the lining around the lung, not from lung tissue itself, and shows nodular pleural thickening on CT with calretinin-positive immunohistochemistry. Lung cancer arises from bronchial or alveolar cells, usually presents as a discrete mass or nodule, and stains TTF-1 positive.

faqs.question: Can mesothelioma be cured?
faqs.answer: Most mesotheliomas are not curable because they are diagnosed at an advanced stage. Selected patients with early-stage epithelioid pleural disease who undergo trimodality therapy or peritoneal disease treated with complete cytoreduction and HIPEC can achieve long-term survival.

faqs.question: What is the best treatment for mesothelioma?
faqs.answer: First-line treatment for unresectable pleural mesothelioma is nivolumab plus ipilimumab based on the CheckMate 743 trial, which extended median overall survival to 18.1 months. Platinum plus pemetrexed chemotherapy remains an alternative.

faqs.question: Is mesothelioma hereditary?
faqs.answer: Most mesotheliomas are sporadic and caused by environmental asbestos exposure. A minority of cases arise in carriers of germline BAP1 mutations, who face a markedly elevated lifetime risk of mesothelioma, uveal melanoma, and renal cell carcinoma.

faqs.question: Can women get mesothelioma?
faqs.answer: Yes. Women account for roughly 20-25% of cases. Female cases are disproportionately due to secondary household asbestos exposure from washing work clothes of an occupationally exposed family member. Peritoneal mesothelioma is relatively more common in women than men.

faqs.question: What jobs carry the highest mesothelioma risk?
faqs.answer: Shipbuilding, insulation, construction, demolition, plumbing, boilermaking, asbestos mining and milling, automotive brake repair, power-plant maintenance, and naval service all carry elevated risk. Among heavily exposed insulation workers, lifetime risk reaches 10%.

faqs.question: How does immunotherapy work in mesothelioma?
faqs.answer: Nivolumab plus ipilimumab blocks the PD-1 and CTLA-4 checkpoints that mesothelioma uses to suppress local immune responses. Blocking these checkpoints reactivates tumor-specific T cells. The CheckMate 743 trial showed median overall survival of 18.1 months versus 14.1 months with chemotherapy.

faqs.question: What is peritoneal mesothelioma?
faqs.answer: Peritoneal mesothelioma is the form that arises in the abdominal lining rather than the chest. It accounts for 10-20% of all mesotheliomas, presents with ascites and abdominal pain, and is often initially misdiagnosed as ovarian or gastrointestinal cancer.

faqs.question: Does smoking cause mesothelioma?
faqs.answer: Smoking alone does not cause mesothelioma. It does multiply the lung-cancer risk in asbestos-exposed individuals roughly 50-fold compared to non-smoker non-exposed controls.

faqs.question: What is BAP1 and why does it matter in mesothelioma?
faqs.answer: BAP1 is a tumor-suppressor gene; somatic loss is found in roughly 60% of pleural mesotheliomas. Germline BAP1 mutations cause an inherited cancer-predisposition syndrome with elevated risk for mesothelioma, uveal melanoma, and renal cell carcinoma.

faqs.question: How is recurrent pleural effusion managed in mesothelioma?
faqs.answer: Talc pleurodesis (instilling sterile talc into the pleural space to fuse the pleural surfaces) succeeds in 70-90% of patients. Indwelling pleural catheters allow ambulatory drainage at home and relieve symptoms in over 90%. The choice depends on lung re-expansion and patient performance status.

faqs.question: Should I get a second opinion for mesothelioma?
faqs.answer: Yes. Mesothelioma diagnosis is histologically nuanced and treatment options vary significantly by center. A second opinion at a high-volume mesothelioma center is recommended for pathology review, staging confirmation, surgical eligibility assessment, and clinical trial access.

faqs.question: Are there clinical trials for mesothelioma?
faqs.answer: Yes. Mesothelioma is an active research area with trials of new checkpoint inhibitors, antibody-drug conjugates targeting mesothelin, oncolytic viruses, CAR-T cell therapies, and PARP inhibitors in BAP1-deficient tumors.

faqs.question: How much does mesothelioma treatment cost?
faqs.answer: Costs vary widely by country and treatment. In the US, first-line nivolumab plus ipilimumab runs into hundreds of thousands of dollars annually without insurance. Pleurectomy/decortication is in the tens of thousands; CRS-HIPEC similar.

faqs.question: Can I claim compensation for mesothelioma?
faqs.answer: Patients with documented asbestos exposure can often pursue compensation through asbestos trust funds, workers compensation, veterans benefits in countries with relevant schemes, or civil litigation against responsible parties.

metaDescription: Mesothelioma: aggressive asbestos-linked cancer of the chest or abdomen lining. Symptoms, diagnosis, immunotherapy, and survival reviewed by our medical board.
